clean_story: Sun’s violent moods.  Earth’s invisible storms. "Space weather" describes the chaotic bursts of energy and particles from the Sun—like a solar temper tantrum—that slam into Earth’s magnetic shield. Imagine the Sun as a boiling pot of soup. Sometimes it spits out bubbles of gas—coronal mass ejections—or flashes X-ray light—solar flares—that race toward us at millions of miles per hour. When they hit, Earth’s magnetic field flexes like a trampoline. The clash creates auroras—the Northern Lights—but also fries satellites, blacks out radio signals, and even trips power grids. In 1859, a solar storm called the Carrington Event set telegraph wires on fire. Today, a repeat could knock out GPS for days, silence emergency radios, or corrupt bank transactions mid-swipe. NASA’s DSCOVR satellite, parked a million miles away, acts like a space weather buoy—giving us 15 to 60 minutes’ warning before a storm hits. Not much time. Three related subjects are magnetic fields, solar cycles, and cosmic rays.
